Created: 260412


## Paces

### windows-setup-first-time-debug (₢A-ABG) [rough]

## Character
Interactive operator-driven iteration. Spec and code both in
motion; BUS*.adoc is working-draft, not load-bearing yet. No
bounded cycle count.

## Goal
First-time Windows setup converges end-to-end on bujn-winpc —
caparison-windows lands, garrison-w lands, workload reach
(jwk/jwc/jws) round-trips. Spec and code in mutual agreement
at close.

## Working posture
Spec edits, bash edits, and live ceremony runs interleave.
Heat-affiliated commits acceptable for spec-only design pivots
that outpace implementation; pace-affiliated when code+spec
land together. Cleanly-separable follow-ons (Cygwin path,
handbook recast, comment annotations, downstream practice)
get their own pace rather than accreting here.

## Done when
Operator declares Windows setup converged with spec and code
coherent against observed Windows OpenSSH behavior on
bujn-winpc.

### revert-wsl-stage-dev-cache (₢A-ABJ) [rough]

## Character
Architectural promotion of a debug-loop accelerator, not a revert.

## Goal
Promote the DEV CACHE shape in `zbujb_caparison_windows_stage_wsl` to
formal architecture under the admin-no-WSL principle: admin produces
the seed tar on disk and never registers rbtww-main in admin's
HKCU\Lxss. Drop garrison-w's `[w-export-seed]` step (which only
existed to export admin's running rbtww-main; admin no longer has
one).

Concretely:
- Remove the DEV CACHE comment-block scaffolding; the shape becomes
  the canonical implementation.
- Decide and document the canonical cache path (currently
  `C:\rbtww-seed.tar`) and where caparison places it: rename if
  warranted, or formalize the current path with rationale.
- Caparison Phase 3 retains [3/6] install + [4/6] export to the
  cache path, and [6/6] unregister of the Ubuntu-24.04 seed; drops
  [5/6] import-into-admin and the tar-removal half of [6/6].
- Garrison-w drops `zbujb_garrison_w_export_seed` and its
  `[w-seed-cleanup]` companion; `[w-session-1/4]` imports directly
  from the canonical cache path.
- Operator must still drive a one-time cache pre-stage on hosts
  where caparison has never produced the tar (or caparison must
  produce it idempotently on first run).

## Done when
- Caparison-windows on a fresh bujn-winpc (or equivalent) lands the
  cache tar at the canonical path and does NOT register rbtww-main
  in admin's HKCU\Lxss (verify: admin's wsl.exe --list does not
  show rbtww-main).
- Garrison-w on the same host imports rbtww-main into workload's
  HKCU\Lxss from the cache tar, without going through any admin-
  side rbtww-main export.
- BUSJCW and BUSJGW specs reflect the admin-no-WSL shape (this
  intersects with ₢A-ABK's scope — coordinate at mount time).

### windows-reboot-primitive-spec-coherence (₢A-ABK) [rough]

## Character
Spec coherence to match unconditional-reboot architecture
landed under ₢A-ABG, plus admin-no-WSL shape landing under ₢A-ABJ.

## Goal
BUS*.adoc specs reflect both the reboot-as-precondition shape and
the admin-no-WSL caparison/garrison restructure:
- BUSJGW gains the reboot step + simplified Phase 2; drops
  [w-export-seed] / [w-seed-cleanup] step descriptions.
- BUSJCW adds a cross-reference noting caparison's role as the
  reboot helper's enabling contract (sshd-at-boot, Tailscale
  autostart, authkeys persistence). Caparison Phase 3 spec
  reflects the admin-no-WSL shape (cache produced, no admin
  registration).
- BUSJCW (or BUSJI as appropriate) adds a paragraph explaining
  why the absolute-path AuthorizedKeysFile in the Match block is
  structurally required, not a Cygwin path organization choice:
  Windows OpenSSH's relative-path resolution via
  GetUserProfileDirectoryW fails on headless accounts (no
  ProfileList registry entry). Cite
  `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md`
  §Chain step 3.
- WSG mints (or doesn't) a WSp item codifying reboot-as-canonical
  -Windows-state-reset for destructive cleanup. Decision recorded
  either way.
- All three documents cross-reference the design memo at
  `Memos/memo-20260511-windows-hive-cleanup-reboot-decision.md`,
  which captures the rejected alternatives (Tier 1 in-process
  unload, Tier 2 handle-hunt, Tier 3 UserProfileSvc cycle, Level 3
  offline NTUSER.DAT injection) and the rationale for the chosen
  primitive. Cross-references prevent the rationale from being
  re-discovered every time someone reads the helper.

## Done when
- BUSJGW step numbering and prose match landed bash.
- BUSJCW cross-references the reboot dependency + admin-no-WSL
  caparison shape.
- BUSJCW (or BUSJI) cites
  `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md` for
  the GetUserProfileDirectoryW rationale behind the absolute-path
  Match block.
- WSG decision recorded (new WSp or "deemed not load-bearing as a
  separate principle").
- All three specs cite `Memos/memo-20260511-windows-hive-cleanup-
  reboot-decision.md` at the appropriate sections.
- Implementation commits under ₢A-ABG (reboot) and ₢A-ABJ (admin-
  no-WSL) cited in spec edits.

### practice-windows-jurisdiction-end-to-end (₢A-AAx) [rough]

## Character
Human-driven with agent amending.

## Docket

End-to-end Windows jurisdiction ceremony on bujn-winpc — exercises
the AAw + AAv work in one continuous operator sitting. Curia-driven
via `tt/buw-jpS` for privileged install steps; garrison ceremonies
run via the standard tabtargets. Agent stands by to amend
`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` diagnostics, handbook prose, or error messages
on any friction.

Sequence:

1. `tt/buw-jpGw bujn-winpc` — AAv pre-flight fires (`rbtww-main`
   absent), emits copy-paste `tt/buw-jpS` install hints.
2. Operator runs the emitted `tt/buw-jpS` lines from the curia. WSL
   kernel + `rbtww-main` distribution provisioned, set as default.
3. `tt/buw-jpGw bujn-winpc` — pre-flight passes; mirrored-identity
   ceremony lands; step 6 knock validates byte-exact privkey
   transport (incidentally covers the BBAA_ base64→openssl
   substitution that the AAm-era docket marked as covered by live
   ceremony). Optional: `tt/buw-jws bujn-winpc` to interactively
   confirm the workload session lands inside `rbtww-main` as the
   WSL Linux user.
4. Operator installs Cygwin POSIX userland with the OpenSSH package
   per vendor docs. Cygwin sshd hardening is operator-manual per
   paddock standing note. RB encodes no Cygwin install detail.
5. `tt/buw-jpGc bujn-winpc` — Cygwin workload ceremony lands; step 6
   knock validates the Cygwin path. Note: garrison-c overwrites the
   workload `authorized_keys` forced command from the wsl.exe shim
   to the cygwin64 shim — workload now routes via Cygwin until
   re-garrisoned with `w`. Optional: `tt/buw-jws bujn-winpc` to
   confirm.
6. Split-HOME probe (Cygwin path): from the curia,
   `ssh -t bujuw_user@rocket 'echo $HOME; getent passwd bujuw_user | cut -d: -f6'`.
   See `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md`
   §Chain step 5 for the chain. If `$HOME` and `getpwnam` disagree,
   flag a follow-on pace (workload privkey planted at
   `z_wlhome/.ssh/id_ed25519` may not be findable via `$HOME`
   expansion for outbound git ops). If they agree (login bash may
   auto-align via `/etc/profile`), close as no-action.

Operator prerequisites per BUS0 standing notes: workload pubkey
pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band, `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`
0600 unencrypted, fenestrate already landed via AAt.

Mid-ceremony stall: if Cygwin install hits intractable vendor
friction, operator can wrap with WSL coverage landed and defer
Cygwin to a follow-on pace. Agent does not auto-split.

Deferred (out-of-scope, named for follow-on): Docker Desktop WSL
integration enable + `docker run --rm hello-world` from the workload
session inside `rbtww-main`. The bare workload-reach validation via
`tt/buw-jws` is the load-bearing test for this pace; Docker
verification follows release-1 docker integration scoping.

Goal: both WSL and Cygwin shell-letter paths exercised end-to-end
against bujn-winpc; mirrored-identity model and Cygwin model both
validated against live Windows OpenSSH; Cygwin split-HOME question
decided.

### bash-spec-link-comments (₢A-AA5) [rough]

## Character
Light comment-only bash touch — preserves chain of custody for review cycles.

## Goal
Bind existing bash function symbols to new spec sections via comment headers without renaming any symbol. Greppability holds still through subsequent review cycles.

## Boundary
- One-line comment near each affected function naming its new spec section (e.g., `# Implements caparison-windows §SSH-trust phases; see BUSJPCw.`).
- Comment annotations on zipper enrollment lines if they help spec→code lookup.
- No symbol renames: functions, constants, env vars, file names all stay.
- `bujb_fenestrate` stays `bujb_fenestrate`. `bujb_garrison_wsl` stays. `BUJB_command_b/c/w` constants stay named as-is.

## Done when
- Every public bash function whose spec home moved or was newly created has a comment header pointing at the new spec.
- bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green.

File-touch bitmap (x = pace commit touched file):

  1 H caparison-windows-decompose
  2 G windows-setup-first-time-debug
  3 D implement-invigilate-all
  4 E extend-busji-operator-precondition-facts
  5 C implement-caparison-windows
  6 F handbook-caparison-windows-recast
  7 B implement-caparison-mac-linux

Created: 260412


## Paces

### windows-setup-first-time-debug (₢A-ABG) [rough]

## Character
Interactive operator-driven iteration. Spec and code both in
motion; BUS*.adoc is working-draft, not load-bearing yet. No
bounded cycle count.

## Goal
First-time Windows setup converges end-to-end on bujn-winpc —
caparison-windows lands, garrison-w lands, workload reach
(jwk/jwc/jws) round-trips. Spec and code in mutual agreement
at close.

## Working posture
Spec edits, bash edits, and live ceremony runs interleave.
Heat-affiliated commits acceptable for spec-only design pivots
that outpace implementation; pace-affiliated when code+spec
land together. Cleanly-separable follow-ons (Cygwin path,
handbook recast, comment annotations, downstream practice)
get their own pace rather than accreting here.

## Done when
Operator declares Windows setup converged with spec and code
coherent against observed Windows OpenSSH behavior on
bujn-winpc.

### revert-wsl-stage-dev-cache (₢A-ABJ) [rough]

## Character
Architectural promotion of a debug-loop accelerator, not a revert.

## Goal
Promote the DEV CACHE shape in `zbujb_caparison_windows_stage_wsl` to
formal architecture under the admin-no-WSL principle: admin produces
the seed tar on disk and never registers rbtww-main in admin's
HKCU\Lxss. Drop garrison-w's `[w-export-seed]` step (which only
existed to export admin's running rbtww-main; admin no longer has
one).

Concretely:
- Remove the DEV CACHE comment-block scaffolding; the shape becomes
  the canonical implementation.
- Decide and document the canonical cache path (currently
  `C:\rbtww-seed.tar`) and where caparison places it: rename if
  warranted, or formalize the current path with rationale.
- Caparison Phase 3 retains [3/6] install + [4/6] export to the
  cache path, and [6/6] unregister of the Ubuntu-24.04 seed; drops
  [5/6] import-into-admin and the tar-removal half of [6/6].
- Garrison-w drops `zbujb_garrison_w_export_seed` and its
  `[w-seed-cleanup]` companion; `[w-session-1/4]` imports directly
  from the canonical cache path.
- Operator must still drive a one-time cache pre-stage on hosts
  where caparison has never produced the tar (or caparison must
  produce it idempotently on first run).

## Done when
- Caparison-windows on a fresh bujn-winpc (or equivalent) lands the
  cache tar at the canonical path and does NOT register rbtww-main
  in admin's HKCU\Lxss (verify: admin's wsl.exe --list does not
  show rbtww-main).
- Garrison-w on the same host imports rbtww-main into workload's
  HKCU\Lxss from the cache tar, without going through any admin-
  side rbtww-main export.
- BUSJCW and BUSJGW specs reflect the admin-no-WSL shape (this
  intersects with ₢A-ABK's scope — coordinate at mount time).

### windows-reboot-primitive-spec-coherence (₢A-ABK) [rough]

## Character
Spec coherence to match unconditional-reboot architecture
landed under ₢A-ABG, plus admin-no-WSL shape landing under ₢A-ABJ.

## Goal
BUS*.adoc specs reflect both the reboot-as-precondition shape and
the admin-no-WSL caparison/garrison restructure:
- BUSJGW gains the reboot step + simplified Phase 2; drops
  [w-export-seed] / [w-seed-cleanup] step descriptions.
- BUSJCW adds a cross-reference noting caparison's role as the
  reboot helper's enabling contract (sshd-at-boot, Tailscale
  autostart, authkeys persistence). Caparison Phase 3 spec
  reflects the admin-no-WSL shape (cache produced, no admin
  registration).
- BUSJCW (or BUSJI as appropriate) adds a paragraph explaining
  why the absolute-path AuthorizedKeysFile in the Match block is
  structurally required, not a Cygwin path organization choice:
  Windows OpenSSH's relative-path resolution via
  GetUserProfileDirectoryW fails on headless accounts (no
  ProfileList registry entry). Cite
  `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md`
  §Chain step 3.
- WSG mints (or doesn't) a WSp item codifying reboot-as-canonical
  -Windows-state-reset for destructive cleanup. Decision recorded
  either way.
- All three documents cross-reference the design memo at
  `Memos/memo-20260511-windows-hive-cleanup-reboot-decision.md`,
  which captures the rejected alternatives (Tier 1 in-process
  unload, Tier 2 handle-hunt, Tier 3 UserProfileSvc cycle, Level 3
  offline NTUSER.DAT injection) and the rationale for the chosen
  primitive. Cross-references prevent the rationale from being
  re-discovered every time someone reads the helper.

## Done when
- BUSJGW step numbering and prose match landed bash.
- BUSJCW cross-references the reboot dependency + admin-no-WSL
  caparison shape.
- BUSJCW (or BUSJI) cites
  `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md` for
  the GetUserProfileDirectoryW rationale behind the absolute-path
  Match block.
- WSG decision recorded (new WSp or "deemed not load-bearing as a
  separate principle").
- All three specs cite `Memos/memo-20260511-windows-hive-cleanup-
  reboot-decision.md` at the appropriate sections.
- Implementation commits under ₢A-ABG (reboot) and ₢A-ABJ (admin-
  no-WSL) cited in spec edits.

### practice-windows-jurisdiction-end-to-end (₢A-AAx) [rough]

## Character
Human-driven with agent amending.

## Docket

End-to-end Windows jurisdiction ceremony on bujn-winpc — exercises
the AAw + AAv work in one continuous operator sitting. Curia-driven
via `tt/buw-jpS` for privileged install steps; garrison ceremonies
run via the standard tabtargets. Agent stands by to amend
`bujb_jurisdiction.sh` diagnostics, handbook prose, or error messages
on any friction.

Sequence:

1. `tt/buw-jpGw bujn-winpc` — AAv pre-flight fires (`rbtww-main`
   absent), emits copy-paste `tt/buw-jpS` install hints.
2. Operator runs the emitted `tt/buw-jpS` lines from the curia. WSL
   kernel + `rbtww-main` distribution provisioned, set as default.
3. `tt/buw-jpGw bujn-winpc` — pre-flight passes; mirrored-identity
   ceremony lands; step 6 knock validates byte-exact privkey
   transport (incidentally covers the BBAA_ base64→openssl
   substitution that the AAm-era docket marked as covered by live
   ceremony). Optional: `tt/buw-jws bujn-winpc` to interactively
   confirm the workload session lands inside `rbtww-main` as the
   WSL Linux user.
4. Operator installs Cygwin POSIX userland with the OpenSSH package
   per vendor docs. Cygwin sshd hardening is operator-manual per
   paddock standing note. RB encodes no Cygwin install detail.
5. `tt/buw-jpGc bujn-winpc` — Cygwin workload ceremony lands; step 6
   knock validates the Cygwin path. Note: garrison-c overwrites the
   workload `authorized_keys` forced command from the wsl.exe shim
   to the cygwin64 shim — workload now routes via Cygwin until
   re-garrisoned with `w`. Optional: `tt/buw-jws bujn-winpc` to
   confirm.
6. Split-HOME probe (Cygwin path): from the curia,
   `ssh -t bujuw_user@rocket 'echo $HOME; getent passwd bujuw_user | cut -d: -f6'`.
   See `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md`
   §Chain step 5 for the chain. If `$HOME` and `getpwnam` disagree,
   flag a follow-on pace (workload privkey planted at
   `z_wlhome/.ssh/id_ed25519` may not be findable via `$HOME`
   expansion for outbound git ops). If they agree (login bash may
   auto-align via `/etc/profile`), close as no-action.

Operator prerequisites per BUS0 standing notes: workload pubkey
pre-registered with GitHub out-of-band, `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`
0600 unencrypted, fenestrate already landed via AAt.

Mid-ceremony stall: if Cygwin install hits intractable vendor
friction, operator can wrap with WSL coverage landed and defer
Cygwin to a follow-on pace. Agent does not auto-split.

Deferred (out-of-scope, named for follow-on): Docker Desktop WSL
integration enable + `docker run --rm hello-world` from the workload
session inside `rbtww-main`. The bare workload-reach validation via
`tt/buw-jws` is the load-bearing test for this pace; Docker
verification follows release-1 docker integration scoping.

Goal: both WSL and Cygwin shell-letter paths exercised end-to-end
against bujn-winpc; mirrored-identity model and Cygwin model both
validated against live Windows OpenSSH; Cygwin split-HOME question
decided.

### bash-spec-link-comments (₢A-AA5) [rough]

## Character
Light comment-only bash touch — preserves chain of custody for review cycles.

## Goal
Bind existing bash function symbols to new spec sections via comment headers without renaming any symbol. Greppability holds still through subsequent review cycles.

## Boundary
- One-line comment near each affected function naming its new spec section (e.g., `# Implements caparison-windows §SSH-trust phases; see BUSJPCw.`).
- Comment annotations on zipper enrollment lines if they help spec→code lookup.
- No symbol renames: functions, constants, env vars, file names all stay.
- `bujb_fenestrate` stays `bujb_fenestrate`. `bujb_garrison_wsl` stays. `BUJB_command_b/c/w` constants stay named as-is.

## Done when
- Every public bash function whose spec home moved or was newly created has a comment header pointing at the new spec.
- bash -n green; tt/buw-st.BukSelfTest.sh green; tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh green.

lastPrompt: Rather, groom A- and propose how to 'freshen' its paddock/dockets.

aiTitle: Search repo for Windows setup documentation

content.text:
Groomed. Here's the state and a concrete freshening proposal.

## Groom overview

**₣A- `rbk-30-win-windows-remote-control`** — **stabled** (your "deferred"), created 260412, ~352 commits of history. **5 remaining `rough` paces:**

| Coronet | Silks | Shape |
|---|---|---|
| **₢A-ABG** | windows-setup-first-time-debug | The live convergence loop |
| **₢A-ABJ** | revert-wsl-stage-dev-cache | Admin-no-WSL seed-cache architecture |
| **₢A-ABK** | windows-reboot-primitive-spec-coherence | Spec catch-up to reboot + admin-no-WSL |
| **₢A-AAx** | practice-windows-jurisdiction-end-to-end | Operator-sitting WSL+Cygwin exercise |
| **₢A-AA5** | bash-spec-link-comments | Comment-only spec↔code binding chore |

## Why it needs freshening — three drifts

1. **Specimen drift.** Every live docket hard-pins the convergence target to **`bujn-winpc`** — ₢A-ABG's "Done when … on bujn-winpc," ₢A-ABJ's "fresh bujn-winpc," and ₢A-AAx literally contains `ssh …@rocket`. rocket is offline; beast is the new specimen. This is exactly the stale-by-mount failure the JJK guide names (exhaustive host pins fail at mount).

2. **Architecture drift — the paddock is behind its own dockets.** The paddock's Caparison **Phase 3** still describes the *old* shape: "Stage rbtww-main … under admin user via wsl --import … always purge-and-reimport." But ₢A-ABJ *promotes the opposite* — admin produces the seed tar and **never** registers rbtww-main. And ₢A-ABK is spec-catch-up for a **reboot primitive** the paddock's ceremony prose doesn't mention at all. So the shape doc describes a ceremony two architectural moves out of date.

3. **Consolidation pressure (your "mess").** It's visible in the file-touch bitmap: the live BURN spine (`burn_regime.sh`, `buw-jpCW`, `BUSJCW`) coexists with residue — `rbhw_windows.sh`/`rbw-HW*`, `buw-HWab/ar/ax`, the pre-rename `burh_*`, and a whole abandoned `BUST*` conscript/discharge/handbook-node track. Nothing cinches what's live vs shed.

## Proposed freshening

### A. Paddock (the shape — draft for your review, not silent edit)

- **Prune coronet refs.** The Standing Notes embed `ABG`/`ABJ`/`ABK`/`AAx` in prose ("fits naturally inside ABG's convergence scope," etc.). JJK posture: coronets never appear in paddock prose — pace-state ops silently invalidate them. Restate by purpose.
- **Drop the "Heat Sequence" section.** It's a silks-list progress journal of completed foundational paces — git log is the journal; the paddock is the shape.
- **Reconcile the ceremony prose** to the admin-no-WSL + reboot-as-precondition shape (or, if those aren't fully cinched yet, add an explicit "Cinched architecture" block stating they're decided and pointing at the two design memos). Right now the decision lives scattered across two rough dockets instead of in the shape.
- **Add a `Live specimen` Standing Note** — beast (RDP-only), rocket offline — and make all ceremony/"done" language **specimen-agnostic** ("a fresh Windows node"). A note can change without re-litigating shape; the pins in dockets cannot.
- **Cinch the BURN/BURP spine + name the residue to shed** (`rbhw*`/`rbw-HW*`/`buw-HW*`/abandoned `BUST*` conscript-discharge). This is what resolves the "mess" — one cinched line declaring the live facility and explicitly retiring the rest.
- **Flip `stabled → racing`** when you're ready to actually work it.

### B. Dockets (reslate)

- **₢A-ABG** — de-pin bujn-winpc → "fresh Windows node (beast as current specimen)." Its scope ("converges end-to-end") is huge; I'd reshape it to the staged shape we discussed (register → handbook → caparison → garrison → knock) and pull the two named defects (WSL_UTF8 UTF-16LE stdout; orphan-profile wildcard cleanup) into explicit boundaries or sibling paces rather than leaving them buried in a paddock note.
- **₢A-ABJ** — de-pin; promote the "operator must one-time pre-stage the cache on a host caparison never touched" line from a footnote to **load-bearing**: beast *is* that host, so first-run seed production is now the critical path, not an optional aside.
- **₢A-ABK** — fix an internal contradiction: its Character says "reboot architecture **landed** under ₢A-ABG," but ABG is still `rough`. Restate the dependency as prospective, not past.
- **₢A-AAx** — most drifted: literal `@rocket`, a fully pre-baked step script (over-prescriptive per docket discipline). I'd either **fold it into ₢A-ABG's** beast exercise or reslate it clean for beast — it shouldn't survive as-is.
- **₢A-AA5** — low priority, but a quick stale-ref fix: its "Done when" cites `tt/rbtd-s.TestSuite.fast.sh`; the current colophon is `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` (worth verifying before edit).

### C. New paces to slate (the build-out you actually want)

- **Node-onboarding sequence + handbook refine** — add the missing "register the node first" front-step to `BUSJHW`, and revive the deferred `buw-hn0` node-landing as the spine ordering register → handbook → caparison → garrison → knock. Offline, pure spec/doc.
- **Register beast** — author `rbmn_nodes/beast/burn.env` + `rbmu_users/bhyslop/beast/burp.env`, validate via `buw-rnv`/`buw-rpv`. Offline.
- *(Optional)* **Residue scope-cinch** — the cleanup that makes the consolidation real.

### D. Reorder

Put the two offline paces (onboarding refine, register beast) **ahead** of the live-exercise paces (₢A-ABG / ₢A-AAx), so heat order = dependency order: offline-first, beast-contact-last.

---

**Recommended sequence to execute:** freshen the **paddock first** (it's the shape everything hangs off — de-pin, reconcile architecture, prune coronets, cinch the spine), *then* reslate ₢A-ABG/ABJ/ABK/AAx against the freshened shape, *then* slate the two new offline paces, *then* rail into order.

Want me to draft the freshened **paddock** for your review as the first move? I'll show it as a full proposed replacement so you can react to the shape before anything is written — and we'll have settled the admin-no-WSL/reboot "is it cinched?" question in the process, since that's the load-bearing call the paddock freshening turns on.

aiTitle: Search repo for Windows setup documentation

message.content: Do we need to reform the shape of paces?

content.text:
Honest answer: **yes — but concentrated on one pace, not a wholesale re-decomposition.** And the question underneath yours is actually "how deep does the reform go," which I'll put to you at the end.

Here's the reasoning. The current 5-pace set was shaped around one organizing principle: **live, unbounded debugging against a reachable rocket.** The work you now want is a different principle: **staged, offline-first BURN/BURP consolidation, validated on beast last.** When the organizing principle shifts, pace shape has to follow — but only where the old shape actually encodes the old principle.

**Where the shape genuinely needs reform:**

- **₢A-ABG is the real problem.** Its Character is *"interactive operator-driven iteration… no bounded cycle count… BUS\*.adoc working-draft, not load-bearing."* It's an unbounded debug blob that bundles caparison + garrison + workload-reach into one live loop. That unboundedness is the direct opposite of your "know precisely what to do" bar — it's a pace shaped to *not* know. Reform = lift every *predictable* (offline) piece out of it, leaving ABG as a single bounded "bring up beast" convergence pace.

- **₢A-AAx should dissolve.** It's a pre-baked operator script (over-prescriptive per docket discipline) hard-wired to `@rocket`. Its WSL+Cygwin-exercise intent folds into the live tranche; it shouldn't survive as a separate scripted pace.

**Where the shape is fine — freshen, don't reform:**

- **₢A-ABJ** (admin-no-WSL cache) and **₢A-ABK** (reboot spec coherence) are clean, well-bounded architecture/spec units. They need de-pinning and a cinch-state decision, not reshaping.
- **₢A-AA5** (spec-link comments) is a fine small chore.

**The strongest reason the shape needs reform isn't that a pace is wrong — it's that the new spine has no home.** The offline BURN/BURP consolidation, the onboarding-landing (`buw-hn0`), the handbook front-step, register-beast — none of that is represented in the current pace set at all. That's the gap reform fills.

**One honest tension I want to name before you decide:** ABG was unbounded *for a reason*. Live Windows OpenSSH is the Pale — foreign, verdict-less behavior you can't predict the cycle-count of. If I over-decompose the *live* part into fake-precise "caparison-lands / garrison-lands / knock-works" paces, they'll bounce rough↔complete every time a live discovery reopens them. So the right reform keeps **one** iteration-tolerant live pace (beast bring-up) and makes everything *offline* genuinely precise — containing the unboundedness to the single unit that earns it, rather than spreading it or pretending it away.

That leaves a real choice about how far to go, which is yours to make:

questions.question: How deep should the reform of ₣A- go?

options.label: Reform paces, keep heat
options.description: Un-blob ₢A-ABG into a bounded beast-bringup pace, dissolve ₢A-AAx, add the offline BURN/BURP tranche, keep ₢A-ABJ/ABK/AA5. Same heat — reuses the paddock's cinched three-verb / BURN/BURP design.

  ── offline tranche (precise) ──
  NEW  onboarding landing + BUSJHW front-step
  ABJ  admin-no-WSL cache       (kept, de-pinned)
  ABK  reboot spec coherence    (kept, de-pinned)
  NEW  register beast (BURN/BURP author+validate)
  NEW  residue scope-cinch      (optional)

  ── live tranche (Pale-facing) ──
  ABG* reshaped: beast bring-up
         caparison → garrison → knock
  AAx  DISSOLVED into ABG*
  AA5  spec-link comments       (kept)

Reuses paddock's cinched design. Residue named
& shelved, not relocated.

options.label: Freshen only, no reshape
options.description: Reslate every docket's text, de-pin rocket→beast, freshen the paddock — but leave the 5-pace structure intact. ₢A-ABG stays an unbounded live loop. Least disruption; the 'mess' and the unbounded blob remain.
options.preview:

  ABG  live debug loop (unbounded)  ← de-pinned
  ABJ  admin-no-WSL cache           ← de-pinned
  ABK  reboot spec coherence        ← de-pinned
  AAx  practice e2e (still scripted)← de-pinned
  AA5  spec-link comments
  + paddock freshened

No shape change. Offline BURN/BURP work has no
pace home. Mess remains.

options.label: Reform the heat itself
options.description: Retire ₣A- (history preserved), nominate a fresh BURN/BURP-node-onboarding heat carrying forward only the live spine. Cleanest break from rocket-era residue — but you recopy the paddock's still-valuable cinched design.

  - reboot spec coherence   (from ABK)
  - register beast
  - beast bring-up          (from ABG)

Clean container. Residue left behind in ₣A-.

message.content: All right, I like that you reminded me that this heat was intended to use rocket to test before we do beast config, and that is why we should defer this heat, keeping that shape.  We won't try to set up beast now.  We can do some cleanup though, I think.  You have done a wide search of my various steps to add windows, so maybe you see some 'dead ends' we ought to cull now?

content.text:
Good — that's the right call, and thank you for the correction: ₣A- is *shaped* to converge against rocket first, so its deferral is principled, not drift. We leave the heat's shape intact and don't touch beast. Cleanup is a separate, safe activity.

Before I propose any culls, I want **evidence**, not just memory — several of these "residue" items may already be gone (post-rename) or may still be quietly live. Culling is destructive, so I'll verify dead-vs-live first, then bring you a categorized proposal to approve. Let me sweep.

input.prompt:
I'm auditing the BUK (Bash Utilities Kit) for DEAD-END residue from superseded Windows-remote-access design attempts, to propose safe deletions. For each candidate cluster below, determine DEAD / LIVE / UNCERTAIN with concrete evidence. "DEAD" means: the file exists but nothing live references it — not enrolled in any zipper (`Tools/buk/buwz_zipper.sh` or similar `*z_zipper.sh`), not dispatched by any workbench (`Tools/buk/buw_workbench.sh`, `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh`, `bujb_cli.sh`), not `include::`'d by any live spec (especially `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc`), and not sourced by any live module. Read-only — do not modify anything.

Investigate these clusters:

1. **burh→burn rename orphans.** The kit renamed a BURH regime to BURN (paces 'burh-to-burn-rename-buk' completed). Check whether any pre-rename artifacts SURVIVED as orphans: files matching `burh_*` (modules), `buw-rh*` tabtargets in `tt/` (e.g. buw-rhk, buw-rhl, buw-rhr, buw-rhv, buw-rhcc/cl/cm/cp/cw/cx), `burh.env`, and any `BURH*` specs. For each that exists, is it referenced anywhere live, or is it an orphan superseded by the `burn_*` / `buw-rn*` equivalents? List the live `buw-rn*` equivalents you find.

2. **Jurisdiction predecessors (fenestrate / WslInstall / access+environment handbook).** The current model is caparison/invigilate/garrison (tabtargets `buw-jpCW/CM/CL`, `buw-jpGb/Gc/Gw`, `buw-jpS`). Check whether these PREDECESSORS survive as orphans: `tt/buw-jpF.Fenestrate.sh` + `BUSJPF-Fenestrate.adoc`; `tt/buw-jpW.WslInstall.sh`; the access/environment handbook tabtargets `tt/buw-HWab/ar/ax/ec/ew/bs/sc/vs.*`; modules `buhw_*.sh`, `buhj_*.sh`, `buw-wcb/wck`, `buw-SI.StationInit.sh`. For each: does it exist, and is it referenced by any live zipper/workbench/spec, or orphaned?

3. **Conscript/Discharge/Inventory specs (rejected design).** The garrison-destructive model explicitly REJECTED conscript/discharge/withdraw/vanquish/inventory verbs ("garrison subsumes them"). Check these spec files in `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/`: BUSTCC-ConscriptCygwin, BUSTCP-ConscriptPowershell, BUSTCW-ConscriptWsl, BUSTGC/GP/GW (Garrison* under BUST namespace — note these differ from the LIVE BUSJGB/GC/GW garrison specs), BUSTHT-HandbookNodeTop, BUSTOE-RemoteRun, BUSTOK-Knock, BUSTOS-InteractiveSession, BUSTWD-Discharge, BUSTWI-Inventory, and any BUSN*-NodeWindows/NodeLinux/NodeMac. CRITICAL: distinguish these from the LIVE BUST* node/privilege regime subdocs (BUSTLL/LR/LV NodeRegime*, BUSTPL/PR/PV PrivilegeRegime*) which ARE include'd in BUS0. For each suspect spec: is it `include::`'d anywhere in BUS0 or any other live .adoc? If not include'd by anything, it's an orphaned spec = DEAD.

Report as a table: file path | exists? | live references (with file:line) | verdict (DEAD/LIVE/UNCERTAIN). Be conservative — if you find ANY live reference, mark LIVE or UNCERTAIN, not DEAD. Flag anything ambiguous explicitly. I will not delete anything without confirming your evidence.

I'm auditing whether the RBK (Recipe Bottle Kit) Windows-handbook track is DEAD residue or a LIVE facility, to decide if it's safe to delete. This is SEPARATE from the BUK jurisdiction handbook (buw-hj*) — I'm asking specifically about the RBK handbook under `Tools/rbk/rbh0/` and its `rbw-*` tabtargets. Read-only — do not modify.

Context: a 'lode' heat reportedly added an action marking "the stale Windows tracks (rbhw*) NOT-available." I need to know if that already happened and whether these are now dead.

Investigate:

1. **RBK Windows handbook modules.** List `Tools/rbk/rbh0/` and identify files matching `rbhw*` (e.g. rbhwb_base.sh, rbhw0_cli.sh, rbhw0_top.sh, rbhwdd_docker_desktop.sh, rbhwdn_docker_wsl_native.sh, rbhwcd_docker_context_discipline.sh). For each: is it sourced/enrolled by a live RBK workbench (`Tools/rbk/...rbw_workbench` or similar) or zipper (`rbz_zipper.sh` / `rbh*` zipper)? 

2. **RBK Windows handbook tabtargets.** In `tt/`, examine `rbw-hw.HandbookWindows.sh`, `rbw-HWdd.DockerDesktop.sh`, `rbw-HWdc.DockerContextDiscipline.sh`, `rbw-HWdw.DockerWSLNative.sh`, and the handbook top `rbw-h0.HandbookTOP.sh`. Are these colophons still enrolled in the live RBK workbench command tables? Open one or two and trace the dispatch.

3. **Is the Windows track marked NOT-available / stale?** Search the RBK handbook code and any onboarding menu (e.g. `rbw-o` ONBOARDING, rbho*/rbhw* base) for logic that marks Windows tracks unavailable, stale, deferred, or NOT-available. Quote what you find (file:line). Did the lode heat already neutralize this track, or is it still wired live?

4. **Onboarding routing.** Does the onboarding entry (`rbw-o` / rbho0_cli.sh / rbhob_base.sh) still ROUTE to the Windows handbook, or has that route been cut? 

5. **Transport working files & superseded memos.** Check for `oq-*.md` files (open-question working files from windows-transport-experiments) anywhere in the repo — are they referenced by anything, or abandoned scratch? Also note (do NOT recommend deleting memos) whether any `Memos/memo-*windows*` or `*wsl*`/`*cygwin*` memo explicitly says it SUPERSEDES or is SUPERSEDED-BY another (consolidation lineage) — just report the lineage.

Report a clear verdict for the RBK rbhw* handbook track: is it DEAD (safe to cull), LIVE (in use), or PARTIALLY-NEUTRALIZED (wired but marked unavailable)? Give evidence with file:line. Be conservative: any live enrollment/route = not DEAD. Flag ambiguity. I will not delete based on verdict alone — I need the evidence trail.

lastPrompt: All right, I like that you reminded me that this heat was intended to use rocket to test before we do beast config, and that is why we should defer this heat, keeping that shape.  We won't try to set…

aiTitle: Search repo for Windows setup documentation

content.text:
Perfect! Now let me create a comprehensive summary of my audit findings:

## AUDIT REPORT: RBK rbhw* Windows Handbook Track Status

### VERDICT: LIVE (in use, partially neutralized via one module deletion)

---

### 1. RBK Windows Handbook Modules: LIVE + PARTIALLY DELETED

**Files in `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbh0/`:**

LIVE modules:
- `rbhwb_base.sh` (kindle/sentinel/base) — **ACTIVE**
- `rbhw0_cli.sh` (CLI furnish) — **ACTIVE**, sources all Windows modules
- `rbhw0_top.sh` (rbhw_top function) — **ACTIVE**, orchestrator for 6-phase setup
- `rbhwht_handbook_top.sh` (rbhw_handbook_top function) — **ACTIVE**, top-level index
- `rbhwdd_docker_desktop.sh` (Docker Desktop procedure) — **ACTIVE**
- `rbhwcd_docker_context_discipline.sh` (Docker context) — **ACTIVE**

DELETED module:
- `rbhwdn_docker_wsl_native.sh` (Native dockerd in WSL) — **DELETED** 2026-05-13
  - File: Deleted in commit `4fb4cf38a` (2026-05-13T14:36:22)
  - Reason: "Sterilize rbw-HWdw (Native dockerd in WSL) ahead of an upcoming WSL handbook rework"
  - Scope: Only the WSL-native dockerd guidance removed; broader WSL plumbing remains

**Enrollment status:** All surviving modules are actively sourced in `rbhw0_cli.sh:47-51`:
- Line 47: `source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbh0/rbhwb_base.sh"`
- Line 48: `source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbh0/rbhwht_handbook_top.sh"`
- Line 49: `source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbh0/rbhw0_top.sh"`
- Line 50: `source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbh0/rbhwdd_docker_desktop.sh"`
- Line 51: `source "${z_rbk_kit_dir}/rbh0/rbhwcd_docker_context_discipline.sh"`

---

### 2. RBK Windows Handbook Tabtargets: LIVE

**Files in `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/tt/`:**

LIVE tabtargets:
- `rbw-hw.HandbookWindows.sh` — **ACTIVE**, main orchestrator
- `rbw-HWdd.DockerDesktop.sh` — **ACTIVE**, Docker Desktop install guide
- `rbw-HWdc.DockerContextDiscipline.sh` — **ACTIVE**, Docker context discipline
- `rbw-h0.HandbookTOP.sh` — **ACTIVE**, links to Windows handbook

DELETED tabtarget:
- `rbw-HWdw.DockerWSLNative.sh` — **DELETED** 2026-05-13
  - Removed in same commit `4fb4cf38a` as the module

**Zipper enrollment status** (`/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbz_zipper.sh:222-228`):
```
buz_group RBZ__GROUP_HANDBOOK   "rbw-HW"  "Handbook — Human-facing procedures"
z_mod="rbh0/rbhw0_cli.sh"
buz_enroll RBZ_HANDBOOK_TOP           "rbw-h0"    "${z_mod}" "rbhw_handbook_top"              ""
buz_enroll RBZ_HANDBOOK_WINDOWS       "rbw-hw"    "${z_mod}" "rbhw_top"                       ""
buz_enroll RBZ_HW_DOCKER_DESKTOP     "rbw-HWdd"  "${z_mod}" "rbhw_docker_desktop"            ""
buz_enroll RBZ_HW_DOCKER_CONTEXT     "rbw-HWdc"  "${z_mod}" "rbhw_docker_context_discipline" ""
```

All four live tabtargets are enrolled and routed through the RBW workbench dispatcher.

---

### 3. Windows Track Marked NOT-available / Stale: PARTIALLY NEUTRALIZED

**BUK Windows handbook:** **FULLY DELETED**
- Commit `e0c50bbfc` (2026-05-05, "Cleanup-prior-attempts sweep")
- Deleted 5 BUK tabtargets: `buw-h0`, `buw-hw`, `buw-HWar`, `buw-HWec`, `buw-HWew`
- Deleted 2 BUK modules: `buhw_cli.sh`, `buhw_windows.sh`
- Commit message: **"prune superseded BUK Windows handbook ecosystem"**
- These were **superseded** by RBK Windows handbook under `rbhw*` and jurisdiction handbook under `buj*`

**RBK Windows handbook:** **PARTIALLY NEUTRALIZED** (1 of 4 modules deleted)
- Commit `4fb4cf38a` (2026-05-13, "Sterilize rbw-HWdw")
- Deleted: `rbhwdn_docker_wsl_native.sh` and `rbw-HWdw.DockerWSLNative.sh`
- Reason: "Narrow scope: only the WSL-native dockerd guidance was sterilized — broader WSL plumbing... remains intact, deferred to a future heat that will redo Windows-Docker handbook content."

**No explicit "NOT-available" markers found** in the code — the deletion was surgical, removing only the WSL-native Docker procedure while preserving the Windows handbook orchestrator itself.

---

### 4. Onboarding Routing: LIVE (Windows route preserved)

**Onboarding entry points:**
- `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbho0_cli.sh` — Onboarding CLI
- `/Users/bhyslop/projects/rbm_alpha_recipemuster/Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbho0_start_here.sh` — Start-here menu

**Result:** The Windows handbook is **NOT routed from onboarding start-here**. The start-here menu (lines 40-150) covers:
- Foundation (Crash Course, Credentials)
- Kludged Crucibles (explorer track, evaluator track)
- Payor + Governor
- Director Subtracks

The Windows handbook (`rbw-hw`) is **accessible separately** via the top-level handbook index (`rbw-h0`), not as a primary onboarding entry point. This is intentional — Windows is a **test infrastructure track**, not a primary onboarding path.

---

### 5. Transport Working Files & Memos: ACTIVE

**Found `oq-*.md` files:** None discovered.

**Windows-related memos found** (all present, none abandoned):
- `memo-20260412-windows-handbook-draft.md` — Draft Windows handbook notes
- `memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md` — Headless account research
- `memo-20260603-windows-docker-desktop-bind-mount.md` — Docker Desktop bind-mount fix
- `memo-20260603-cygwin-ifrit-first-run-conntrack.md` — Cygwin attack suite findings
- `memo-20260601-wsl-skirmish-first-pass.md` — WSL test results
- `memo-20260511-windows-hive-cleanup-reboot-decision.md` — Windows hive cleanup
- `memo-20260508-windows-transport-experiments.md` — Transport layer research
- `memo-20260510-windows-transport-ps5-anti-rationalization.md` — PowerShell transport analysis
- `memo-20260511-windows-transport-wrapper-postlude-synthesis.md` — Transport wrapper synthesis
- `memo-20260517-windows-substrate-landscape-for-theurge.md` — Theurge Windows integration

**No explicit supersession lineage found** in memo headers, though commit messages reference "deferred to a future heat that will redo Windows-Docker handbook content."

---

### EVIDENCE TRAIL

**Key commits establishing current state:**

1. **e0c50bbfc** (2026-05-05)
   - "Cleanup-prior-attempts sweep: prune superseded BUK Windows handbook ecosystem"
   - Deleted all BUK Windows handbook (`buw-hw*`) files — these were **superseded** by RBK
   - RBK Windows handbook (`rbhw*`) **preserved** with surgical edits only

2. **825a1e8ce** (2026-05-05)
   - "L4 RBK Windows orchestrator rewire to fenestrate + garrison + handbook"
   - Rewired 6-phase setup sequence reflecting jurisdiction split
   - All existing `rbhw*` modules remain **LIVE**

3. **4fb4cf38a** (2026-05-13)
   - "Sterilize rbw-HWdw (Native dockerd in WSL)"
   - Deleted `rbhwdn_docker_wsl_native.sh` (the WSL native Docker procedure only)
   - Explicitly deferred broader WSL rework: "deferred to a future heat that will redo Windows-Docker handbook content"
   - Verification: "bash -n clean on rbz_zipper.sh, rbhw0_cli.sh, rbhw0_top.sh"

---

### CONCLUSION

**The RBK `rbhw*` Windows handbook track is LIVE, with ONE SURGICAL DELETION:**

- **NOT DEAD** — The core Windows handbook orchestrator (`rbhw0_top`, `rbhw_handbook_top`, tabtargets `rbw-hw`, `rbw-h0`) are actively enrolled in the RBK workbench and fully functional.
- **PARTIALLY NEUTRALIZED** — The WSL native Docker procedure (`rbhwdn_docker_wsl_native.sh` / `rbw-HWdw`) was deliberately removed on 2026-05-13 as a narrow sterilization, not a full deprecation. The commit explicitly preserves broader WSL plumbing for a future rework.
- **SUPERSEDED** — The earlier BUK Windows handbook (`buw-hw*`) was fully replaced by RBK Windows handbook (`rbhw*`) on 2026-05-05, with all load-bearing content migrated to the RBK modules.

**Safe to delete?** No. The surviving `rbhw*` modules and tabtargets are actively wired into the RBK workbench dispatcher and serve as the project's Windows test infrastructure orchestration guide. Deleting them would break the handbook index (`rbw-h0`) and the Windows setup orchestrator (`rbw-hw`).

aiTitle: Search repo for Windows setup documentation

content.text:
Here's the honest result — and it's not the result I expected when I started looking: **there's essentially nothing to cull. Your prior cleanup paces already did it.** Almost everything I flagged as "residue" in earlier turns turns out not to exist on disk.

## What the evidence shows

| Cluster I suspected | Reality |
|---|---|
| **Pre-rename `burh_*` / `buw-rh*` / `BURH*`** | **Gone.** burh→burn rename left zero orphans; only live `burn_*` / `buw-rn*` remain. |
| **Rejected conscript / discharge / inventory specs** (`BUSTCC/CW/GC/GW/HT/OE/OK/OS/WD/WI`, `BUSN*`) | **Never committed** (or already removed). Not on disk. The only `BUST*` files are the **live** regime subdocs (`BUSTLL/LR/LV`, `BUSTPL/PR/PV`), all `include::`'d in BUS0. |
| **Fenestrate / WslInstall predecessors** (`buw-jpF`, `BUSJPF`, `buw-jpW`) | **Gone** — superseded by caparison, already pruned. |
| **BUK access/environment handbook** (`buw-HW*`, `buhw_*`) | **Gone** — deleted 2026-05-05 in the "cleanup-prior-attempts sweep" (commit `e0c50bbfc`, msg: *"prune superseded BUK Windows handbook ecosystem"*). |
| **`oq-*.md` transport scratch files** | **Gone.** |

The only suspects that *do* exist are **live**: `buhj_cli.sh`/`buhj_jurisdiction.sh` (back the `buw-hjw` handbook), `buw-SI.StationInit.sh` (referenced by JJK fundus + RBK qualify), and the BUST* regime specs. Nothing orphaned.

The one detail that looks like a leftover but is **by design**: the `BUSJIW/IM/IL` invigilate specs are live in BUS0 but have no `buw-jpIW/IM/IL` tabtargets — because, per the paddock, *"Invigilate has no tabtarget — it is invoked only internally."* Correct as-is.

## A correction I owe you

In an earlier turn I told you the RBK `rbw-hw` handbook was a "stale lineage, don't follow it." **That was wrong** — `rbw-hw` / `rbhw*` is **live and enrolled** in the RBK workbench (`rbz_zipper.sh`), routing through `rbw-h0`. What I'd actually seen was a *planned, not-yet-executed* lode pace (**₢A-ABB**, still `rough`) that intends to mark the `rbhw*` tracks not-available. So today it's live; the neutralization is future intent, not done. Only one surgical removal has happened there — the WSL-native-docker piece (`rbw-HWdw`/`rbhwdn_*`), deleted 2026-05-13. I'm sorry for the overstatement.

## So where's the "mess" you feel?

It's **conceptual, not janitorial.** There isn't a litter of dead files — there are **two *live* Windows handbook lineages at adjacent layers**:

- **BUK** `BUSJHW` / `buw-hjw` — operator-manual *node reachability* (OpenSSH, Tailscale, the caparison prerequisites).
- **RBK** `rbw-hw` / `rbhw*` — Recipe Bottle *Docker test-infrastructure* onboarding (Docker Desktop, context discipline).

They don't overlap-and-rot; they sit one above the other (get the node reachable → get Docker working for RB tests). That's the spread that *feels* like mess. But merging or re-bordering them is a **design-consolidation decision** for a future heat — not a deletion I can responsibly make now, and not a dead end.

## My recommendation

**Cull nothing today** — there's no safe, obvious dead file to remove, and your past self already swept the genuine residue. Inventing deletions here would be the opposite of cleanup.

Two optional follow-ups if you want to keep pulling the thread (neither needed):
1. **A general orphan-tabtarget scan** — every file in `tt/` with no `*z_zipper.sh` enrollment, repo-wide (beyond the windows scope). That's the one place a *genuine* surprise dead-end could still hide, and I haven't checked outside the windows tracks.
2. **Memo thinning** — the 10 windows/wsl/cygwin memos are all archival and worth keeping, but `memo-20260412-windows-handbook-draft.md` is the most historical (it documents the now-replaced draft). I'd keep it, but if you ever want to thin the archive, that's the first candidate — your call, not mine.

Want either, or shall we let it rest here?

content: Goal: set up the new Windows box "beast" without rocket. We decided to keep heat ₣A- deferred (it needs rocket first) and audited for cullable Windows residue. The audit found nothing dead to delete. Next: you decide whether to stop here or run a broader orphan-tabtarget scan.

message.content: Aww.  Memos are harmless, all told.  Yes, definitely conceptual... But I don't often load you with all this info and would love to bank a positive durable benefit from your work in assembling this context.  Any suggestions based off all you have seen?

content.text:
The most durable thing to bank is the one that cost the most to produce this session: **the map itself.** I walked a sprawling subsystem from cold — and right now that map lives only in this conversation, which evaporates when it closes. The honest highest-value move is to write it across the gap so the next instance (or you, six weeks out) starts where we *ended* instead of re-running the fan-out.

Here's what I'd suggest, ranked.

## 1. (Recommended) A Windows remote-node *orientation map*

A single short doc that is strictly a **pointer-map, not a restatement** — built only from the references that *don't* rot (file paths, spec/concept names, the heat firemark, cinched rationale), never the kind that do (line numbers, step counts, host pins). The irony isn't lost on me: we just audited residue, so the one rule is *don't birth new stale residue.* Kept as a map, it can't.

What it would capture, all of which this session established and none of which is written down in one place:

- **The spine:** BURN/BURP is the clean facility; three verbs — caparison (admin posture) / invigilate (read-only verify) / garrison (workload account). Everything else hangs off that.
- **The bring-up sequence:** operator-manual handbook → caparison → garrison → workload knock. The entry points: `BUSJHW` via `tt/buw-hjw` (the first-time keyboard/RDP handbook), `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md` (the headless-SSH bible), `WSG` (transport discipline).
- **Why ₣A- is parked:** deferred *by design* — the heat is shaped to converge against rocket (a known box) first; beast is a future specimen; rocket isn't strictly required in principle, but the heat's shape prefers proving against the known host. That rationale is genuinely new this session and recorded nowhere.
- **The one open conceptual question:** two *live* handbook lineages at adjacent layers — BUK `BUSJHW`/`buw-hjw` (node reachability) vs RBK `rbw-hw`/`rbhw*` (Docker test-infra onboarding). Not dead, not redundant — a future border/merge decision.
- **The reassurance:** residue already culled; the "mess" is conceptual, not janitorial.

The real choice is **where to home it**, because that sets *when it loads*:
- a **standalone memo** (`Memos/memo-...-windows-node-orientation.md`) — loaded on demand, zero standing weight;
- a compact **context-file block** (in the rbk/buk context that's `@`-included) — loaded *every* session, maximal legibility, but it adds standing weight to what's already a large CLAUDE.md;
- the existing **CLAUDE.md "Test Environments"** section already has a `bujn-winpc`/rocket entry — a few durable lines there is the lightest-touch option.

## 2. (Small complement) One paddock Standing Note on ₣A-

You asked to keep the heat's shape — this *honors* that while banking the single cinched decision we reached: a one-line "why deferred" note (rocket-first convergence; beast future specimen). It's the paddock's proper job (cinched rationale, not progress), it's conversation-sanctioned since we just had the conversation, and it stops the next mount from re-deriving why it's parked.

## 3. (Optional) An itch for the consolidation question

The two-handbook-lineage question is exactly the unease you named. Capturing it as a JJK itch turns recurring vague discomfort into tracked future work with a name — cheap insurance against re-discovering it cold.

---

My lean: **#1 as a standalone memo, plus #2.** The memo banks the whole map at zero standing cost; the paddock note banks the one decision where it'll actually be seen on re-entry. #3 only if the consolidation itch feels worth tracking to you.

Want me to draft #1 for your review? If so, tell me which home — and I'll write it as a pure pointer-map so it ages well.

input.content:
# Windows Remote-Node Bring-Up — Orientation Map

*Pointer-map, not a procedure. Written 2026-06-04 (Claude Opus 4.8) at the
close of a context-assembly session, so a future instance starts from the map
instead of re-running the search.*

**Durability discipline:** this file references only things that don't rot —
file paths, spec/concept names, the heat firemark, cinched rationale. If you
edit it, keep it that way: no line numbers, no step counts, no host-state, no
version strings. The moment it restates content instead of pointing at it, it
becomes the residue it was meant to prevent.

## What this covers

Reaching a remote node (especially a Windows host) and provisioning it
deterministically: the BUK **jurisdiction** facility. Logical-only umbrella (no
AXLA entity); implementation seat `Tools/buk/bujb_jurisdiction.sh` (+
`bujb_cli.sh`). Spec spine: `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/BUS0-BashUtilitiesSpec.adoc`
§Remote Node Access, with the `BUSJ*` sub-spec family.

## The spine: BURN / BURP

Two regimes, joined by one investiture identity. This is the clean, settled
part of the facility — when in doubt, consolidate toward it.

- **BURN** — node shape (host + OS family). Per-node, git-tracked.
  `rbmm_moorings/rbmn_nodes/<investiture>/burn.env`, fields `BURN_HOST` +
  `BURN_PLATFORM`. Tabtargets `buw-rnl/rnr/rnv` (List/Render/Validate). Module
  `burn_regime.sh` / `burn_cli.sh`.
- **BURP** — privileged + workload credentials. Per-station-user.
  `rbmm_moorings/rbmu_users/<user>/<investiture>/burp.env`, fields
  `BURP_PRIVILEGED_USER`, `BURP_PRIVILEGED_KEY_FILE`, `BURP_WORKLOAD_KEY_FILE`.
  The `<investiture>` dir name must match a BURN profile (enforced at load).
  Tabtargets `buw-rpl/rpr/rpv`. Module `burp_regime.sh` / `burp_cli.sh`.

## Three verbs

- **Caparison** — admin host posture, per platform. Nuclear (purge-and-
  re-establish; no skip-if-present). Tabtargets `buw-jpCW/CM/CL`. Specs
  `BUSJCW/CM/CL`.
- **Invigilate** — read-only posture verification. **No tabtarget by design** —
  invoked internally (caparison post-check, garrison precondition). Specs
  `BUSJIW/IM/IL`.
- **Garrison** — workload account provisioning, per shell-letter (b = bash,
  c = Cygwin, w = WSL). First step is invigilate. Tabtargets `buw-jpGb/Gc/Gw`.
  Specs `BUSJGB/GC/GW`.

Workload reach once garrisoned: `buw-jwk` (knock), `buw-jwc` (command file),
`buw-jws` (interactive). Privileged pass-through: `buw-jpS`.

## First-time Windows bring-up sequence

1. **Operator-manual scope** (at the keyboard / over RDP — nothing can reach the
   box yet): `BUSJHW-HandbookWindows.adoc` via `tt/buw-hjw`. OpenSSH server,
   firewall, temporary password auth, the security-sensitive registry edits
   (`DevicePasswordLessBuildVersion`, `PlatformAoAcOverride`), Tailscale install
   + Run-Unattended + first-auth, netplwiz auto-login. The node self-auths to
   the tailnet here — **no other node is required.**
2. **Register the node** — author `burn.env` + `burp.env` for the new
   investiture; validate with `buw-rnv` / `buw-rpv`. (No dedicated onboarding
   landing exists yet — see "Known gaps.")
3. **Caparison** (operator station → host over SSH) — `buw-jpCW <investiture>`.
4. **Garrison** — `buw-jpGw <investiture>` (and/or `buw-jpGc`).
5. **Knock** — `buw-jwk <investiture>` to confirm workload reach.

## Essential references

- **Headless-SSH bible:** `Memos/memo-20260516-windows-headless-account-anatomy.md`
  — the net-user-through-SSH-reachable chain; the absolute-path
  `AuthorizedKeysFile` Match block (GetUserProfileDirectoryW fails on headless
  accounts), Cygwin split-HOME, StrictModes ACLs. Required reading before
  touching account internals.
- **Transport discipline:** `Tools/buk/vov_veiled/WSG-WindowsScriptingGuide.md`
  — the cmd.exe → PowerShell / Cygwin / WSL stack, per-letter escape rules, the
  wrapper/postlude shape.
- **Reboot-as-state-reset rationale:**
  `Memos/memo-20260511-windows-hive-cleanup-reboot-decision.md`.
- **Substrate landscape (Cygwin vs WSL for theurge):**
  `Memos/memo-20260517-windows-substrate-landscape-for-theurge.md`.

## Two live handbook lineages (the conceptual "mess")

Not dead, not redundant — adjacent layers. A future border/merge decision, not
a deletion:

- **BUK jurisdiction handbook** — `BUSJHW` / `buw-hjw` (+ `buw-hj0` top,
  `buw-hjm/hjl`). Scope: get the *node reachable and provisioned* (the
  caparison/garrison prerequisites above).
- **RBK Windows handbook** — `tt/rbw-hw` / `tt/rbw-h0`, modules
  `Tools/rbk/rbh0/rbhw*`. Scope: Recipe Bottle *Docker test-infrastructure*
  onboarding (Docker Desktop, context discipline). The native-dockerd-in-WSL
  piece was sterilized; the rest is live.

## The heat: ₣A-

`₣A- rbk-30-win-windows-remote-control` — **stabled (deferred)**. Live state is
authoritative via `jjx_show ₣A-`; do not trust any pace list copied here (it
would rot).

**Why deferred (cinched):** the heat is shaped to converge first-time Windows
setup against a *known* host — `bujn-winpc` (tailnet `rocket`) — before
generalizing to fresh specimens. Standing up a brand-new node does **not**
strictly require the old host (the node model is self-contained: BURN profile +
local provisioning + Tailscale self-auth), but the convergence design prefers
proving the ceremony against the known box first. It stays parked until the
operator elects to converge it — against rocket, or a deliberately-adopted new
specimen.

**Architecture in flight** (carried by the heat's live paces — concept names,
look them up via `jjx_show ₣A-`): *admin-no-WSL seed cache* (caparison produces
the `rbtww-main` seed tar; admin never registers the distro) and *reboot-as-
canonical-Windows-state-reset*. Container runtime for release-1 is Docker
Desktop for Windows; native dockerd-in-WSL is deferred (shared-kernel iptables).

## Known gaps (named, not yet built)

- **No node-onboarding landing.** The first-time sequence above is assembled
  from separate entry points; there's no single "register + bring up a new node"
  front door. A `HandbookNode` landing was designed once and not built.
- **Handbook assumes the node is already registered** — `BUSJHW` references the
  BURP user / BURN hostname but doesn't tell you to author the profiles first.

## Residue status

Already culled. The superseded Windows attempts (BUK `buw-hw*` handbook
ecosystem, `burh_*` pre-rename artifacts, fenestrate/WslInstall predecessors,
the rejected conscript/discharge/inventory specs) were removed by prior cleanup
paces. As of this writing there are **no orphaned files** in the Windows tracks
— the remaining spread is conceptual (the two lineages above), not janitorial.
